{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3471785]] Wikidata name: Asama shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Fuji Sankei Mandara from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha.png|right|300px|thumb|Fuji [[Mandala|Mandara]] from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Jinja]] An {{nihongo|'''Asama shrine'''|浅間神社|Asama jinja, Sengen jinja}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto Shrine]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] centered on the worship of the ''[[kami]]'' of volcanos in general, and [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] in particular.<ref name="studies454">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p.454.</ref>
Per the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honchō]], there are approximately 1300 Asama shrines in the country, centered primarily in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], and to a lesser extent in the prefectures of the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] and [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi]]. Almost all Asama Shrines are within sight of Mount Fuji. Where this is not possible because of distance or obstructions, a miniature replica of Mount Fuji known as a {{nihongo|''Fujizuka''|富士塚}} made from rocks from the mountain was often erected within the shrine grounds. The head of all Asama shrines is the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], located in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]].<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html] Fujisan Hongū Sengentaisha (official website)</ref>
In 2013 the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], [[:en:Yamamiya Sengen-jinja|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]], Murayama Sengen Shrine, Suyama Sengen Shrine, Fuji Sengen Shrine, Kawaguchi Asama Shrine, and Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine were added to the [[:en:World Heritage List|World Heritage List]] as part of the Fujisan Cultural Site.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/multiple=1&unique_number=1883|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration: Maps|work=World Heritage|publisher=UNESCO|accessdate=2013-07-04}}</ref>
==Shinto belief== The primary ''[[kami]]'' at Asama shrines is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], sometimes in combination with her father, [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and/or sister [[Iwanaga-hime]]. Konohanasakuya-hime appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' and appears to have originally associated with protection against fire, per the stories which appear in both chronicles. However, there is no specific reference which explains how she came to be associated with [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] at some point in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi]] or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="studies454"/><ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 457.</ref>
==History== The derivation of the word “Asama” is subject to considerable uncertainty and debate, but the original meaning of the word appears to be connected with volcanoes or volcanic eruptions, and the presence of water springs in the foothills of such mountains. Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|[[:en:Mount Asama|Mount Asama]]|浅間山|Asama-san}} in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]] and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie]] appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[:en:Asama Maru|Asama Maru]] * [[Fujiko (religion)|Fujiko]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== *[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html Fujisan Hongu Sengentaisha] (official website) *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084 Encyclopedia of Shinto] *[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm Japanese architecture and art]

Per the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honchō]], there are approximately 1300 Asama shrines in the country, centered primarily in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], and to a lesser extent in the prefectures of the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] and [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi]]. Almost all Asama Shrines are within sight of Mount Fuji. Where this is not possible because of distance or obstructions, a miniature replica of Mount Fuji known as a {{nihongo|''Fujizuka''|富士塚}} made from rocks from the mountain was often erected within the shrine grounds. The head of all Asama shrines is the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], located in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]].<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html] Fujisan Hongū Sengentaisha (official website)</ref>
In 2013 the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], [[:en:Yamamiya Sengen-jinja|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]], Murayama Sengen Shrine, Suyama Sengen Shrine, Fuji Sengen Shrine, Kawaguchi Asama Shrine, and Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine were added to the [[:en:World Heritage List|World Heritage List]] as part of the Fujisan Cultural Site.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/multiple=1&unique_number=1883|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration: Maps|work=World Heritage|publisher=UNESCO|accessdate=2013-07-04}}</ref>
==Shinto belief== The primary ''[[kami]]'' at Asama shrines is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], sometimes in combination with her father, [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and/or sister [[Iwanaga-hime]]. Konohanasakuya-hime appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' and appears to have originally associated with protection against fire, per the stories which appear in both chronicles. However, there is no specific reference which explains how she came to be associated with [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] at some point in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi]] or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="studies454"/><ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 457.</ref>
==History== The derivation of the word “Asama” is subject to considerable uncertainty and debate, but the original meaning of the word appears to be connected with volcanoes or volcanic eruptions, and the presence of water springs in the foothills of such mountains. Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|[[:en:Mount Asama|Mount Asama]]|浅間山|Asama-san}} in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]] and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie]] appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[:en:Asama Maru|Asama Maru]] * [[Fujiko (religion)|Fujiko]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.

==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]] [[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]] [[it:Santuario di Asama]] [[ja:浅間神社]] [[sv:Asama-helgedom]] [[zh:淺間神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3471785}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3471785]] Wikidata name: Asama shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Fuji Sankei Mandara from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha.png|right|300px|thumb|Fuji [[Mandala|Mandara]] from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Jinja]] An {{nihongo|'''Asama shrine'''|浅間神社|Asama jinja, Sengen jinja}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto Shrine]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] centered on the worship of the ''[[kami]]'' of volcanos in general, and [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] in particular.<ref name="studies454">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p.454.</ref>
Per the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honchō]], there are approximately 1300 Asama shrines in the country, centered primarily in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], and to a lesser extent in the prefectures of the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] and [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi]]. Almost all Asama Shrines are within sight of Mount Fuji. Where this is not possible because of distance or obstructions, a miniature replica of Mount Fuji known as a {{nihongo|''Fujizuka''|富士塚}} made from rocks from the mountain was often erected within the shrine grounds. The head of all Asama shrines is the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], located in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]].<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html] Fujisan Hongū Sengentaisha (official website)</ref>
In 2013 the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], [[:en:Yamamiya Sengen-jinja|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]], Murayama Sengen Shrine, Suyama Sengen Shrine, Fuji Sengen Shrine, Kawaguchi Asama Shrine, and Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine were added to the [[:en:World Heritage List|World Heritage List]] as part of the Fujisan Cultural Site.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/multiple=1&unique_number=1883|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration: Maps|work=World Heritage|publisher=UNESCO|accessdate=2013-07-04}}</ref>
==Shinto belief== The primary ''[[kami]]'' at Asama shrines is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], sometimes in combination with her father, [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and/or sister [[Iwanaga-hime]]. Konohanasakuya-hime appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' and appears to have originally associated with protection against fire, per the stories which appear in both chronicles. However, there is no specific reference which explains how she came to be associated with [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] at some point in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi]] or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="studies454"/><ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 457.</ref>
==History== The derivation of the word “Asama” is subject to considerable uncertainty and debate, but the original meaning of the word appears to be connected with volcanoes or volcanic eruptions, and the presence of water springs in the foothills of such mountains. Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|[[:en:Mount Asama|Mount Asama]]|浅間山|Asama-san}} in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]] and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie]] appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3471785]] Wikidata name: Asama shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Fuji Sankei Mandara from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha.png|right|300px|thumb|Fuji [[Mandala|Mandara]] from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Jinja]] An {{nihongo|'''Asama shrine'''|浅間神社|Asama jinja, Sengen jinja}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto Shrine]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] centered on the worship of the ''[[kami]]'' of volcanos in general, and [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] in particular.<ref name="studies454">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p.454.</ref>
Per the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honchō]], there are approximately 1300 Asama shrines in the country, centered primarily in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], and to a lesser extent in the prefectures of the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] and [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi]]. Almost all Asama Shrines are within sight of Mount Fuji. Where this is not possible because of distance or obstructions, a miniature replica of Mount Fuji known as a {{nihongo|''Fujizuka''|富士塚}} made from rocks from the mountain was often erected within the shrine grounds. The head of all Asama shrines is the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], located in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]].<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html] Fujisan Hongū Sengentaisha (official website)</ref>
In 2013 the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], [[:en:Yamamiya Sengen-jinja|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]], Murayama Sengen Shrine, Suyama Sengen Shrine, Fuji Sengen Shrine, Kawaguchi Asama Shrine, and Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine were added to the [[:en:World Heritage List|World Heritage List]] as part of the Fujisan Cultural Site.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/multiple=1&unique_number=1883|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration: Maps|work=World Heritage|publisher=UNESCO|accessdate=2013-07-04}}</ref>
==Shinto belief== The primary ''[[kami]]'' at Asama shrines is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], sometimes in combination with her father, [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and/or sister [[Iwanaga-hime]]. Konohanasakuya-hime appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' and appears to have originally associated with protection against fire, per the stories which appear in both chronicles. However, there is no specific reference which explains how she came to be associated with [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] at some point in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi]] or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="studies454"/><ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 457.</ref>
==History== The derivation of the word “Asama” is subject to considerable uncertainty and debate, but the original meaning of the word appears to be connected with volcanoes or volcanic eruptions, and the presence of water springs in the foothills of such mountains. Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|[[:en:Mount Asama|Mount Asama]]|浅間山|Asama-san}} in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]] and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie]] appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[:en:Asama Maru|Asama Maru]] * [[Fujiko (religion)|Fujiko]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== *[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html Fujisan Hongu Sengentaisha] (official website) *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084 Encyclopedia of Shinto] *[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm Japanese architecture and art]

By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[:en:Asama Maru|Asama Maru]] * [[Fujiko (religion)|Fujiko]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3471785]] Wikidata name: Asama shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Fuji Sankei Mandara from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha.png|right|300px|thumb|Fuji [[Mandala|Mandara]] from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Jinja]] An {{nihongo|'''Asama shrine'''|浅間神社|Asama jinja, Sengen jinja}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto Shrine]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] centered on the worship of the ''[[kami]]'' of volcanos in general, and [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] in particular.<ref name="studies454">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p.454.</ref>
Per the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honchō]], there are approximately 1300 Asama shrines in the country, centered primarily in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], and to a lesser extent in the prefectures of the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] and [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi]]. Almost all Asama Shrines are within sight of Mount Fuji. Where this is not possible because of distance or obstructions, a miniature replica of Mount Fuji known as a {{nihongo|''Fujizuka''|富士塚}} made from rocks from the mountain was often erected within the shrine grounds. The head of all Asama shrines is the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], located in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]].<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html] Fujisan Hongū Sengentaisha (official website)</ref>
In 2013 the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], [[:en:Yamamiya Sengen-jinja|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]], Murayama Sengen Shrine, Suyama Sengen Shrine, Fuji Sengen Shrine, Kawaguchi Asama Shrine, and Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine were added to the [[:en:World Heritage List|World Heritage List]] as part of the Fujisan Cultural Site.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/multiple=1&unique_number=1883|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration: Maps|work=World Heritage|publisher=UNESCO|accessdate=2013-07-04}}</ref>
==Shinto belief== The primary ''[[kami]]'' at Asama shrines is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], sometimes in combination with her father, [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and/or sister [[Iwanaga-hime]]. Konohanasakuya-hime appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' and appears to have originally associated with protection against fire, per the stories which appear in both chronicles. However, there is no specific reference which explains how she came to be associated with [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] at some point in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi]] or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="studies454"/><ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 457.</ref>
==History== The derivation of the word “Asama” is subject to considerable uncertainty and debate, but the original meaning of the word appears to be connected with volcanoes or volcanic eruptions, and the presence of water springs in the foothills of such mountains. Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|[[:en:Mount Asama|Mount Asama]]|浅間山|Asama-san}} in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]] and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie]] appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[:en:Asama Maru|Asama Maru]] * [[Fujiko (religion)|Fujiko]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== *[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html Fujisan Hongu Sengentaisha] (official website) *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084 Encyclopedia of Shinto] *[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm Japanese architecture and art]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3471785]] Wikidata name: Asama shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Fuji Sankei Mandara from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha.png|right|300px|thumb|Fuji [[Mandala|Mandara]] from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Jinja]] An {{nihongo|'''Asama shrine'''|浅間神社|Asama jinja, Sengen jinja}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto Shrine]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] centered on the worship of the ''[[kami]]'' of volcanos in general, and [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] in particular.<ref name="studies454">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p.454.</ref>
Per the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honchō]], there are approximately 1300 Asama shrines in the country, centered primarily in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], and to a lesser extent in the prefectures of the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] and [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi]]. Almost all Asama Shrines are within sight of Mount Fuji. Where this is not possible because of distance or obstructions, a miniature replica of Mount Fuji known as a {{nihongo|''Fujizuka''|富士塚}} made from rocks from the mountain was often erected within the shrine grounds. The head of all Asama shrines is the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], located in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]].<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html] Fujisan Hongū Sengentaisha (official website)</ref>
In 2013 the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], [[:en:Yamamiya Sengen-jinja|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]], Murayama Sengen Shrine, Suyama Sengen Shrine, Fuji Sengen Shrine, Kawaguchi Asama Shrine, and Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine were added to the [[:en:World Heritage List|World Heritage List]] as part of the Fujisan Cultural Site.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/multiple=1&unique_number=1883|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration: Maps|work=World Heritage|publisher=UNESCO|accessdate=2013-07-04}}</ref>
==Shinto belief== The primary ''[[kami]]'' at Asama shrines is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], sometimes in combination with her father, [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and/or sister [[Iwanaga-hime]]. Konohanasakuya-hime appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' and appears to have originally associated with protection against fire, per the stories which appear in both chronicles. However, there is no specific reference which explains how she came to be associated with [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] at some point in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi]] or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="studies454"/><ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 457.</ref>
==History== The derivation of the word “Asama” is subject to considerable uncertainty and debate, but the original meaning of the word appears to be connected with volcanoes or volcanic eruptions, and the presence of water springs in the foothills of such mountains. Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|[[:en:Mount Asama|Mount Asama]]|浅間山|Asama-san}} in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]] and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie]] appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[:en:Asama Maru|Asama Maru]] * [[Fujiko (religion)|Fujiko]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== *[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html Fujisan Hongu Sengentaisha] (official website) *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084 Encyclopedia of Shinto] *[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm Japanese architecture and art]

Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|[[:en:Mount Asama|Mount Asama]]|浅間山|Asama-san}} in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]] and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie]] appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})

[https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3471785]] Wikidata name: Asama shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Fuji Sankei Mandara from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha.png|right|300px|thumb|Fuji [[Mandala|Mandara]] from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Jinja]] An {{nihongo|'''Asama shrine'''|浅間神社|Asama jinja, Sengen jinja}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto Shrine]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] centered on the worship of the ''[[kami]]'' of volcanos in general, and [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] in particular.<ref name="studies454">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p.454.</ref>
Per the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honchō]], there are approximately 1300 Asama shrines in the country, centered primarily in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], and to a lesser extent in the prefectures of the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] and [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi]]. Almost all Asama Shrines are within sight of Mount Fuji. Where this is not possible because of distance or obstructions, a miniature replica of Mount Fuji known as a {{nihongo|''Fujizuka''|富士塚}} made from rocks from the mountain was often erected within the shrine grounds. The head of all Asama shrines is the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], located in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]].<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html] Fujisan Hongū Sengentaisha (official website)</ref>
In 2013 the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], [[:en:Yamamiya Sengen-jinja|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]], Murayama Sengen Shrine, Suyama Sengen Shrine, Fuji Sengen Shrine, Kawaguchi Asama Shrine, and Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine were added to the [[:en:World Heritage List|World Heritage List]] as part of the Fujisan Cultural Site.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/multiple=1&unique_number=1883|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration: Maps|work=World Heritage|publisher=UNESCO|accessdate=2013-07-04}}</ref>
==Shinto belief== The primary ''[[kami]]'' at Asama shrines is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], sometimes in combination with her father, [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and/or sister [[Iwanaga-hime]]. Konohanasakuya-hime appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' and appears to have originally associated with protection against fire, per the stories which appear in both chronicles. However, there is no specific reference which explains how she came to be associated with [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]] at some point in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi]] or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="studies454"/><ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 457.</ref>
==History== The derivation of the word “Asama” is subject to considerable uncertainty and debate, but the original meaning of the word appears to be connected with volcanoes or volcanic eruptions, and the presence of water springs in the foothills of such mountains. Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|[[:en:Mount Asama|Mount Asama]]|浅間山|Asama-san}} in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]] and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie]] appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired [[:en:Emperor Toba|Emperor Toba]] as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]], and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]s and [[:en:Bosatsu|Bosatsu]].<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujiyoshida|Fujiyoshida]], Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in [[:en:Fuji, Shizuoka|Fuji]], Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in [[:en:Nara (city)| Nara]], a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in [[:en:Bunkyō|Bunkyō]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Numazu|Numazu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[:en:Asama Maru|Asama Maru]] * [[Fujiko (religion)|Fujiko]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|WD=Q9441|lt=The Buddha}}s and {{ill|Bosatsu|en|Bosatsu|WD=Q23925162|lt=Bosatsu}}.<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida}}, Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida}}, Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in {{ill|Fuji, Shizuoka|en|Fuji, Shizuoka|WD=Q328613|lt=Fuji}}, Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt= Nara}}, a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}} *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in {{ill|Shinjuku|en|Shinjuku|WD=Q179645|lt=Shinjuku}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in {{ill|Shinjuku|en|Shinjuku|WD=Q179645|lt=Shinjuku}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in {{ill|Bunkyō|en|Bunkyō|WD=Q212713|lt=Bunkyō}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in {{ill|Numazu|en|Numazu|WD=Q241037|lt=Numazu}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Jinja Honchō" to "Association of Shinto Shrines"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sengen shrine]][[fa:زیارتگاه سنگن]][[fr:Sanctuaires Asama]][[it:Santuario di Asama]][[ja:浅間神社]][[sv:Asama-helgedom]][[zh:淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3471785]] Wikidata name: Asama shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Fuji Sankei Mandara from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha.png|right|300px|thumb|Fuji [[Mandala|Mandara]] from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Jinja]] An {{nihongo|'''Asama shrine'''|浅間神社|Asama jinja, Sengen jinja}} is a type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} centered on the worship of the ''[[kami]]'' of volcanos in general, and {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} in particular.<ref name="studies454">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p.454.</ref>
Per the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]], there are approximately 1300 Asama shrines in the country, centered primarily in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, and to a lesser extent in the prefectures of the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} and {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi}}. Almost all Asama Shrines are within sight of Mount Fuji. Where this is not possible because of distance or obstructions, a miniature replica of Mount Fuji known as a {{nihongo|''Fujizuka''|富士塚}} made from rocks from the mountain was often erected within the shrine grounds. The head of all Asama shrines is the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], located in {{ill|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|en|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|WD=Q496072|lt=Fujinomiya, Shizuoka}}.<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html] Fujisan Hongū Sengentaisha (official website)</ref>
In 2013 the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], [[:en:Yamamiya Sengen-jinja|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]], Murayama Sengen Shrine, Suyama Sengen Shrine, Fuji Sengen Shrine, Kawaguchi Asama Shrine, and Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine were added to the [[:en:World Heritage List|World Heritage List]] as part of the Fujisan Cultural Site.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/multiple=1&unique_number=1883|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration: Maps|work=World Heritage|publisher=UNESCO|accessdate=2013-07-04}}</ref>
==Shinto belief== The primary ''[[kami]]'' at Asama shrines is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], sometimes in combination with her father, [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and/or sister [[Iwanaga-hime]]. Konohanasakuya-hime appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' and appears to have originally associated with protection against fire, per the stories which appear in both chronicles. However, there is no specific reference which explains how she came to be associated with {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} at some point in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi}} or {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.<ref name="studies454"/><ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 457.</ref>
==History== The derivation of the word “Asama” is subject to considerable uncertainty and debate, but the original meaning of the word appears to be connected with volcanoes or volcanic eruptions, and the presence of water springs in the foothills of such mountains. Mountain-worship based cults centered on {{nihongo|{{ill|Mount Asama|en|Mount Asama|WD=Q242730|lt=Mount Asama}}|浅間山|Asama-san}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma}} and {{nihongo|Mount Asama|朝熊山|Asama-yama}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie}} appear contemporary with the mountain-cult centered on Mount Fuji, via references in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}}''.<ref>{{cite book | title = The Manyoshu: A New and Complete Translation| author = Honda, H. H. (tr.)| publisher = Hokuseido Press, Tokyo| year = 1967}}</ref> However, worship of Mount Fuji, as the tallest and most famous volcano in Japan came to dominate. Mount Fuji has erupted eighteen times in recorded history. In order to pacify it, the Imperial Court awarded it [[:en:Ritsuryo|court Rank]] and venerated it as ''Sengen Ōkami'' in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}
According to shrine tradition from the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] moved an existing shrine from the slopes of Mount Fuji to the lowlands during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 458.</ref> Traditions also exist associating Mount Fuji with immortality-seeking wizards, and attribute the legendary mystical powers of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}} to his training on the mountain.
From the Heian period, the worship of the volcano ''kami'' as providers of water combined with [[Shingon]] esoteric Buddhism and with [[Shugendō]] practices. ''[[Yamabushi]]'' Matsudai Shōnin is said to have climbed Mount Fuji several hundred times and built a temple, with the retired {{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} as his patron.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084] Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref>
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, pilgrimages to climb Mount Fuji increased in popularity, and [[mandala]] were produced both as souvenirs, and to spread the cult. Such mandala typically depicted pilgrims landing at {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|en|Miho no Matsubara|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara}}, and the various stages of the ascent of Mount Fuji. The top of the mountain is depicted as having three peaks, about which float various {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|WD=Q9441|lt=The Buddha}}s and {{ill|Bosatsu|en|Bosatsu|WD=Q23925162|lt=Bosatsu}}.<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm] Japanese architecture and art </ref> In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the ''Fuji-kō'', a religious confraternity system became extremely popular in the Kantō region, using [[magico-religious]] practices with talismans to protect followers from illness and catastrophe, despite efforts by the authorities to discourage it.
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the cult of Mount Fuji declined precipitously, and the ''Fuji-ko'' groups are now subsumed into various of the sect Shintō organizations.
==Related shrines== {{dynamic list}} *Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (一宮浅間神社) in [[:en:Fuefuki, Yamanashi|Fuefuki]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} *Omuro Sengen Shrine (小室浅間神社) in {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida}}, Yamanashi Prefecture *Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine (北口本宮冨士浅間神社) in {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida}}, Yamanashi Prefecture *Fujiomuro Sengen Shrine (冨士御室浅間神社) in [[:en:Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi|Fujikawaguchiko]], Yamanashi Prefecture *[[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] (静岡淺間神社) in {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} *Fujirokusho Sengen Shrine (富知六所淺間神社) in {{ill|Fuji, Shizuoka|en|Fuji, Shizuoka|WD=Q328613|lt=Fuji}}, Shizuoka Prefecture *Asamasha (浅間社) in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt= Nara}}, a Side Shrine of Naramichi Ten Jinja (奈良町天神社), {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}} *Geino Asama Shrine (芸能浅間神社) in {{ill|Shinjuku|en|Shinjuku|WD=Q179645|lt=Shinjuku}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} *Naruko Fuji Asama Shrine (成子富士浅間神社) in {{ill|Shinjuku|en|Shinjuku|WD=Q179645|lt=Shinjuku}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} *Fuji Asama Ookami (富士浅間大神) in {{ill|Bunkyō|en|Bunkyō|WD=Q212713|lt=Bunkyō}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} *Asama Jinja (浅間神社) in [[:en:Susono, Shizuoka|Susono]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} ({{coord|35|13|14.1|N|138|53|10.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark}}) *Sengen Jinja (浅間神社) in {{ill|Numazu|en|Numazu|WD=Q241037|lt=Numazu}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} ({{coord|35|07|26.2|N|138|52|22.4|E|region:JP_type:landmark}})
==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|List of Jingū|en|List of Jingū|WD=Q120680118|lt=List of Jingū}} * [[:en:Asama Maru|Asama Maru]] * [[Fujiko (religion)|Fujiko]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== *[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html Fujisan Hongu Sengentaisha] (official website) *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1084 Encyclopedia of Shinto] *[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/f/fujimandara.htm Japanese architecture and art]
